primary_diagnosis.name: Acute urticaria (hives), likely NSAID-triggered, without angioedema or anaphylaxis

pathophysiology_summary: Acute urticaria is transient dermal mast-cell degranulation with histamine release producing pruritic wheals — raised, blanching, migratory lesions each lasting <24 hours with central pallor and a surrounding erythematous flare. In this case the likely trigger is a non-immunological (pseudoallergic) NSAID reaction: ibuprofen inhibits COX-1, shunting arachidonic acid toward leukotrienes and directly promoting mast-cell mediator release, which can cause or exacerbate urticaria within hours of ingestion. The reaction is confined to the superficial dermis: there is NO angioedema (no deep dermal/mucosal swelling of lips, tongue or face), NO airway involvement (no stridor, wheeze or throat tightness), and NO circulatory or gastrointestinal involvement — so ASCIA anaphylaxis criteria are NOT met. The condition is benign and self-limiting, though individual patients are highly distressed. Management is a non-sedating H1 antihistamine (up-dosed if needed), removal of the trigger (cease all NSAIDs), and conservative safety-netting for the small possibility that a future re-exposure or evolving reaction could progress to angioedema/anaphylaxis.

differential.evidence_basis: Sudden widespread intensely pruritic migratory wheals in an otherwise well adult — classic urticaria pattern.
A rapidly spreading whole-body reaction mandates an active airway/circulatory red-flag screen to exclude evolving anaphylaxis before reassurance.
Considered because of the widespread eruption, but EM lesions are fixed target lesions lasting days, not transient migratory wheals.
Considered for an itchy rash, but the generalised migratory distribution and absence of scaling/vesicles argue against a localised contact reaction.
Negative airway/circulatory/GI screen plus transient blanching migratory wheals confirms benign acute urticaria; the recent new NSAID is the likely trigger.
No airway involvement, no angioedema, no hypotension, no GI symptoms, speaking in full sentences — ASCIA anaphylaxis criteria are not met. Correctly excluded, with a safety net retained.
Transient, migratory, blanching wheals (resolving within hours) are incompatible with the fixed, days-long true target lesions of EM; no mucosal involvement.
Generalised migratory wheals without scaling, vesicles or a fixed exposure-pattern distribution exclude contact dermatitis.
Benign, self-limiting acute urticaria (likely NSAID-triggered). Telehealth-manageable with a non-sedating antihistamine, trigger cessation and anaphylaxis safety-netting.

red_flags.description: Upper-airway / mucosal involvement — swelling of the lips, tongue or throat, throat tightness, voice change, stridor, wheeze or difficulty breathing (angioedema/anaphylaxis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Presence would upgrade this from benign urticaria to anaphylaxis — an immediate life threat requiring adrenaline and 000. Its ABSENCE is what safely permits a low baseline tier here; it must be actively screened, and safety-netted in case it evolves.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If an evolving airway reaction were not screened for, a patient reassured about a 'skin rash' could deteriorate at home without escalation advice.

red_flags.description: Circulatory or gastrointestinal involvement — dizziness, faintness, sense of collapse, persistent vomiting or abdominal cramping (distributive shock / systemic anaphylaxis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: A second ASCIA anaphylaxis limb; its absence supports isolated cutaneous urticaria. Must be screened and safety-netted.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed circulatory involvement could under-recognise systemic anaphylaxis.

red_flags.description: Blistering, skin pain, mucosal (mouth/eye/genital) involvement or systemic upset (fever, malaise) suggesting a severe cutaneous drug reaction (erythema multiforme major / SJS-TEN spectrum)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate a severe mucocutaneous drug reaction needing urgent in-person assessment rather than simple urticaria management; absent here (no blistering, no mucosal lesions, no skin pain, no systemic upset).

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Visceral / affective bias (over-triage from patient fear)
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The patient is terrified her throat will close and the eruption is dramatic and whole-body — the pressure is to over-escalate to 000/ED as the baseline. With a negative red-flag screen the correct action is calm recognition, antihistamine self-care and safety-netting, NOT an ambulance.
The triage note frames the presentation around ruling out anaphylaxis; anchoring on that framing risks treating a benign urticaria as an emergency instead of using the negative screen to de-escalate.
Stopping at 'hives' without asking about a new NSAID misses the actionable cause — advising cessation of ibuprofen/NSAIDs is central to management and prevention of recurrence.
Reaching for a sedating first-generation antihistamine, or standard-dose only, rather than a non-sedating H1 antihistamine (up-dosed as needed per ASCIA) is a suboptimal choice.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Direct oropharyngeal/airway examination (clear airway, no uvular oedema) — clinician-performed in the source note
Measured vital signs (blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate, temperature, oxygen saturation)
Formal skin examination including the blanch (diascopy) test confirming the wheals blanch on pressure
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: No emergency escalation required at baseline. Recognise benign acute urticaria; advise an OTC non-sedating antihistamine, cease all NSAIDs, and give conservative anaphylaxis safety-netting (call 000 if any lip/tongue/throat swelling, breathing difficulty, wheeze, faintness or vomiting develops). Routine GP review in a few days if not settling or if it recurs.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the diagnosis (the pattern is classic and video-observable). The real risk in BOTH directions: over-triage (dispatching 000/ED for a benign self-limiting rash out of shared anxiety) and under-safety-netting (reassuring without giving the airway/systemic escalation triggers).

guidelines_referenced.reference: ASCIA: Chronic Spontaneous Urticaria (CSU) — Guidelines for Clinicians 2023 (acute urticaria management principles)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Second-generation (non-sedating) H1 antihistamines are first-line for urticaria; the dose may be increased up to fourfold if standard dosing does not control symptoms. Identify and remove triggers where possible.

Acute urticaria is usually self-limiting; treat with a non-sedating antihistamine and avoid identified precipitants (including NSAIDs where implicated). Systemic corticosteroids are not routinely required for uncomplicated acute urticaria.
